author: @blerg
text:
Another free option is to import your image to Inkscape, and rastering it(SHIFT-ALT-B), and choosing color or brightness cutoff. This way you're left with an SVG or DXF you can scale as you please - all in tiny lines I may add, so processing time might be long depending on the image. 

After creating the raster, delete the original image from Inkscape, and save the file in Inkscape as a DXF, making sure to check ROBO-lines and DXF-lines when the dialogue pops up. They're the only two options. You can also choose to save as SVG and add it directly to your laser-program, but I've had more success with DXF-format. 

Import the DXF into laser software - I use autolaser. 

Choose what lines are to be cut, what to carve, etc., and run the job :)

author: @scantrain5007
text:
Dear Luke,
it's nice to see what you can do with a laser mashine. 
I really don't know the limits and materials you can work with.
However: To burn an image on a wooden board is a nice start of it. 
Questions: Can you burn images on metals like copper, stainless steel? How is the behaviour on different plasic materials?

replies.author: @blerg
replies.text:
Hello, I've spent a couple hundred hours laser engraving and cutting, so here are some answers:

1. You can engrave metals, but they may need to be sprayed with something if the surface is too smooth, causing reflectance. There's lots of options once you start googling ;)

2. Plastics are very hazardous. Make sure it's laser-safe. It's easy to buy laser-safe acryllic, just ask in the store, or check forums. We had someone try to engrave what he thought was acryllic, but was treated with polyeurethane on top, and ended up coughing up blood - very serious. Certain types of plastics release very toxic chemicals when burnt, among them, mustard-gas, so be very careful.

3. Be careful with wood as well - make sure it's untreated, or just oiled lightly with some form of vegetable-oil. Stains and finishing oils may contain combustible chemicals, which can cause your material to incinerate, or at worst start a fire. 

4. There are lots of tutorials on youtube, so I'd suggest watching a few vids here first to build some confidence :) 

5. Happy crafting ;)

replies.author: @scantrain5007
replies.text: @blerg Many thanks for your comment. I been not aware about dangers - good to know!

author: @hkitservices
text:
You should play with the speed of the lases as well to give you more range in your color ratio.
You could also remove the background on the image, so the subject of the image is more in focus, or light up the background
